The LiteSpeed Cache vs WP Rocket debate is usually framed as a fair fight between two plugins. That framing hides the one fact that actually decides it: which layer the cache engine runs on.
WP Rocket is an excellent premium plugin that runs inside WordPress, through PHP. LiteSpeed Cache is a free plugin that hands caching down to the LiteSpeed web server itself. On a LiteSpeed host, that structural difference matters more than any feature checklist — and it leads somewhere most affiliate reviews never go.
It also has real money attached. WP Rocket’s single-site license is $59 per year, and that is the renewal price too. LiteSpeed Cache is free on any LiteSpeed server. So the real question isn’t only which is faster, but whether you should pay for a plugin to do a job your server may already do better, for nothing.
Two engines, two layers
Both plugins do full-page caching: they store a finished HTML snapshot of each page so visitors don’t wait for WordPress and PHP to rebuild it on every request. The difference is where that snapshot lives and who serves it.
WP Rocket operates entirely inside WordPress. It writes static HTML and serves it through rewrite rules, but WordPress and PHP still load to deliver a cached page. That’s exactly why it works on almost any host — it never depends on the web server’s own caching abilities.
LiteSpeed Cache hands the actual caching down to the LiteSpeed server. The plugin is mostly a control panel; the server stores and serves. When a cached page is requested, LiteSpeed answers it before WordPress or PHP start. The flip side: install LSCache on Apache or Nginx and the page-caching engine has nothing to bind to. Its other tools still run, but the headline advantage disappears.
The PHP-Layer Tax
Here’s the concept that reframes the whole comparison: the PHP-Layer Tax — the extra work a cached request performs when the cache engine sits above PHP instead of below it.
Trace a single cached page view. With WP Rocket, the request hits the server, PHP starts, WordPress boots, the plugin intercepts, and the snapshot returns — five steps, and a PHP worker is occupied the whole time. With LiteSpeed Cache, the request hits the server and the cached page returns — two steps, no PHP worker touched.
That occupied worker is the part that bites. On shared WordPress hosting, concurrency is capped by PHP workers (entry processes). A server-level cache serves repeat visitors without spending one, so a traffic spike that would exhaust a worker-limited plan under WP Rocket can pass quietly under LSCache. It’s invisible on a speed-test screenshot and very visible under load.
The decision, in one row
The trade-off comes down to a short list of dimensions that actually change your outcome:

• Where caching runs — LiteSpeed Cache: server level, below PHP. WP Rocket: application level, above PHP.

• Annual cost — LiteSpeed Cache: free. WP Rocket: $59/year.

• PHP worker per cached hit — LiteSpeed Cache: none. WP Rocket: one.

• Under heavy traffic — LiteSpeed Cache serves cache before PHP; WP Rocket pays the PHP-Layer Tax.

• Best fit — LiteSpeed Cache: anyone on a LiteSpeed host. WP Rocket: sites on non-LiteSpeed stacks.
It collapses to a single question. If your host runs LiteSpeed, LiteSpeed Cache gives you the structurally faster engine for free. If it doesn’t, WP Rocket is genuinely excellent and worth its price. The decision is made by your server.
Where WP Rocket still wins
In fairness, there are three cases where WP Rocket remains the right call. First, if your host isn’t LiteSpeed — it’s universally compatible and excellent on Apache or Nginx. Second, if you manage many client sites across mixed stacks and want one familiar interface. Third, if you specifically need its Remove Unused CSS workflow.
But notice that every one of those is the plugin compensating for the server. The moment the server itself runs LiteSpeed, the compensation isn’t needed — and you’d be paying $59 a year to add a PHP-layer cache on top of a faster server-layer cache you already have.
The $59 question, reframed
So the most useful way to read this isn’t “which plugin should I buy.” It’s “which layer should my cache run on.” A reader on a non-LiteSpeed host weighing a $59/year plugin has a third option: move to a LiteSpeed host, get the faster server-level engine free, and drop the recurring plugin cost at the same time.
For context, on production AHosting accounts the median cached Time To First Byte on the LiteSpeed stack is about 16 milliseconds, versus 700–1,400 ms uncached. That gap is the PHP-Layer Tax expressed in real numbers — and because cached pages never spend a PHP worker, the same model protects the headroom that keeps a site up during a surge.

The full guide — including the complete cache layer decision matrix and an interactive cost calculator — is on the AHosting blog.
— Matt Chrust, Director of Business Development, AHosting
